title: Fathers, Hope, and Working-Class Men's Discontent
gist: Grant Martsolf and Grant Bailey use the Global Flourishing Study (200,000+ participants, 22 countries) to argue that the well-being gap between working-class and college-educated American men is rooted not just in economics but in childhood family conditions — above all the father-son relationship, which explains nearly a third of the hope gap.

insights.claim: The share of U.S. men aged 25-44 who are 'not too happy' rose from 11% in the 1980s to 24% recently, with non-college men far worse off (27%) than college-educated men (17%), per the General Social Survey.
insights.speaker: Grant Martsolf, Grant Bailey
insights.so_what: Documents a widening class-by-happiness gap, a structural source of social and political discontent.
insights.quote: the share of men ages 25 to 44 who are “not too happy” has risen from 11% in the 1980s to 24% in the most recent years
insights.locator: Data from the General Social Survey (GSS)

insights.claim: The suicide rate per 100,000 white non-Hispanic men aged 25-44 rose from 31 in 1992 to 54 in 2023, a 71% increase over three decades, part of rising 'deaths of despair.'
insights.speaker: Grant Martsolf, Grant Bailey
insights.so_what: Hard mortality data underscoring the severity of the male despair trend beyond self-reported happiness.
insights.quote: the suicide rate per 100,000 white non-Hispanic men ages 25 to 44 has risen from 31 in 1992 to 54 in 2023, a shocking 71% increase

insights.claim: The share of working-class men aged 25-44 who are married fell from 73% to 42% between 1980 and 2025, and the share married with children at home fell from 61% to just 30%, per the Current Population Survey.
insights.speaker: Grant Martsolf, Grant Bailey
insights.so_what: Quantifies the collapse of stabilizing institutions (marriage, fatherhood) among non-college men, the demographic backdrop to the despair data.
insights.quote: The share of working-class men ages 25 to 44 who are married fell from 73% to 42% from 1980 to 2025
insights.locator: In 1980, men in their prime working years

insights.claim: Compared to college-educated peers, young working-class men aged 25-44 are 17 points less likely to have grown up with married parents, 11 points less likely to have had a good relationship with their father, and 10 points less likely to have felt loved by their father.
insights.speaker: Grant Martsolf, Grant Bailey
insights.so_what: Shows class differences in adult well-being trace back to measurable childhood family deficits, not only adult economics.
insights.quote: working-class men are 17 percentage points less likely to have grown up with married parents
insights.locator: We find that young-working class men ages 25 to 44

insights.claim: Childhood family variables jointly explain 18% to 33% of the well-being gap between college-educated and young working-class men, with hope the most explained outcome.
insights.speaker: Grant Martsolf, Grant Bailey
insights.so_what: Sizes the family contribution to the class well-being gap, justifying family-focused (not purely economic) interventions.
insights.quote: significantly explained jointly by the family variables, ranging from 18% to 33 percent
insights.locator: We find that differences in well-being

insights.claim: Across all models the father relationship is the single most significant explanatory variable, explaining nearly one-third of the hope difference between college-educated and working-class young men.
insights.speaker: Grant Martsolf, Grant Bailey
insights.so_what: Isolates the father-son bond as the highest-leverage childhood factor for adult male hope — a precise target for family policy and ministry.
insights.quote: the relationship with their father emerges as arguably the single most influential factor shaping their sense of hope

insights.claim: The class well-being gap is pronounced among younger men but largely disappears among men aged 45+, where working-class and college-educated men report similar scores — suggesting either an age or cohort effect.
insights.speaker: Grant Martsolf, Grant Bailey
insights.so_what: The disparity may partly resolve as young men accumulate stabilizing resources, tempering alarmist readings of the data.
insights.quote: Among men ages 45 and older, working-class and college-educated men report relatively similar well-being scores.

insights.claim: The authors caution that overall effect sizes are small and warn against overstating the plight of working-class men as a whole, even while flagging meaningful age-stratified differences.
insights.speaker: Grant Martsolf, Grant Bailey
insights.so_what: Analytic honesty marker — the signal is real but modest, relevant to weighting the policy claims.
insights.quote: care should be taken not to overstate the plight of working-class men as a whole
